dialogue.line: Oh! Marie! Hey!
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie! Long time no see!
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know, it's been weeks. How are you?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good, good. Busy, but good. You?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same. I needed a coffee, so here I am.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Me too. The queue is long today.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It always is on Mondays.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: True. Hey, do you have time? Want to sit together?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah, I have fifteen minutes. Let's grab a table.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Perfect.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You run into an old friend at the supermarket. You haven't seen them for a long time. Start a short conversation — greet them, ask how they are, and invite them for a coffee.

phrase_cle.example: — Hi Marie! Long time no see! — Hey Sophie, yeah, it's been ages!
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень распространённое дружеское приветствие, когда вы не видели человека какое-то время. Буквально — 'давно не видел'. Используется между друзьями и хорошими знакомыми, не в формальной обстановке.

content.cultural_note: In British and English-speaking cultures, small talk — short, friendly conversation about everyday things (weather, how busy you are, where you're going) — is very important when you meet someone. It's a way to show warmth before anything more serious. You don't need to share big news; 'busy, but good' is a perfect answer.

key_vocabulary.note: короткий ответ вместо 'My situation is the same'
британский вариант; в США чаще говорят 'the line'

comprehension_questions.hint: In a coffee shop — specifically, in the queue.
comprehension_questions.question: Where does this conversation take place?

comprehension_questions.hint: A few weeks — Sophie says 'it's been weeks'.
comprehension_questions.question: How long has it been since Sophie and Marie last saw each other?
Does Sophie have time to sit down with Marie?

dialogue.line: Hi Marie, it's me. What are you up to?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Just having a cuppa. You?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same. Still in my pyjamas.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Classic Saturday.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Any plans today?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Not really. Maybe a walk in the park.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Want to come with me? I need to pop to the shops.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah, alright. What time?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Give me an hour. I need a shower first.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Perfect. See you at eleven.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: It's Saturday morning. Call a friend and suggest doing something together. Ask what they're up to, share your plans, and invite them along.
prompt_phrases: Hi! What are you up to?

phrase_cle.example: — Hi! What are you up to? — Not much, just having coffee. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Супер-распространённая неформальная фраза у британцев. Буквально — 'до чего ты дошёл?', но значит просто 'чем занят, какие планы?'. Используется постоянно между друзьями — по телефону, в сообщениях, при встрече.

content.cultural_note: Tea is a genuine part of British daily life — not a cliché. Brits drink around 100 million cups a day. 'A cuppa' means a cup of tea (never coffee), usually with milk. If a Brit offers you tea, it's a sign of warmth and welcome. Refusing can seem cold — at least say 'maybe later!'

key_vocabulary.note: очень британское; 'cup of tea' в разговоре
часто используется вместо 'I need an hour'

comprehension_questions.hint: She's having a cup of tea — 'a cuppa'.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie doing when Sophie calls?
What does Sophie need to do before meeting?

comprehension_questions.hint: At eleven — Sophie said 'give me an hour'.
comprehension_questions.question: What time are they meeting?

dialogue.line: Marie? Fancy seeing you here!
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Sophie! Oh, hi! I didn't see you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Doing your shopping?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Yeah, just a few bits. You?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same. I needed milk and bread.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Classic. That's always the list.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh, are these strawberries any good?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: They look a bit dodgy, to be honest.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Right, I'll skip them then.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good call. See you at the tills?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You run into a friend in a supermarket. React with surprise, make small talk about what you're both buying, and ask for advice on something.

phrase_cle.example: — Oh! Sophie! Fancy seeing you here! — I know, what are the odds?
phrase_cle.explanation: Используется, когда случайно сталкиваешься со знакомым в неожиданном месте. Дружелюбно, с лёгким удивлением. 'Fancy' здесь — старое значение 'представить себе'. Очень британская фраза.

content.cultural_note: Tesco is the biggest supermarket chain in the UK — you'll find one on nearly every high street. Brits often go shopping for 'just a few bits' rather than a big weekly shop. 'Dodgy' is an incredibly useful word: it can describe food that's past its best, a suspicious person, or a questionable area. Master it and you'll sound properly British.

comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie need to buy?

comprehension_questions.hint: Marie says they look 'dodgy' — not fresh or good quality.
comprehension_questions.question: Why doesn't Sophie buy the strawberries?

dialogue.line: Ugh, it's packed.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Monday morning at Oxford Circus. What do you expect?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How long till the next one?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Two minutes. Look at the board.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh, right. I never look up.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Here it comes. Brace yourself.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Please mind the gap...
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Classic. Let's squeeze on.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Which stop do you need?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Bond Street. One stop. You?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You're on a crowded tube platform with a friend. Complain about the crowd, check how long until the next train, and say which stop you need.
prompt_phrases: How long till the next one?

phrase_cle.example: — Please mind the gap between the train and the platform. — Every time. I love it.
phrase_cle.explanation: Знаменитое объявление в лондонском метро — его слышишь на каждой станции. 'Mind' здесь = 'be careful of'. За пределами метро фраза стала культурным символом Лондона: её печатают на футболках и сумках.

content.cultural_note: The London Underground — or 'the Tube' — opened in 1863 and is the oldest metro in the world. Rush hour (7:30–9:30am and 5–7pm) is legendary: people pack in so tightly you don't need to hold on. Oxford Circus is one of the busiest stations. 'Mind the gap' has been announced since 1969 and is now one of London's most iconic phrases.

comprehension_questions.hint: At Oxford Circus tube station, on a Monday morning.
comprehension_questions.question: Where are Sophie and Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Two minutes — Marie points to the board.
comprehension_questions.question: How long until the next train?

dialogue.line: Marie, it's me. I'm running late.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh no. How late?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Fifteen minutes. The bus was cancelled.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Typical. Where are you now?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Still at the bus stop. I'm so sorry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don't worry. I'll grab a coffee and wait.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You're a star. I'll be there as fast as I can.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Take it easy. I'm not going anywhere.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Thanks, Marie. See you in a bit.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: See you soon.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You're going to be late for a meeting with a friend. Call them, explain why, say how late you'll be, and apologise.

phrase_cle.example: — Sorry, I'm running late. Can we make it ten minutes later? — No worries, see you then.
phrase_cle.explanation: Самый вежливый способ сказать, что ты опаздываешь. 'Running' не значит 'бежать' — это просто устойчивое выражение. Используется в сообщениях, звонках, письмах. Звучит профессионально и дружелюбно одновременно.

content.cultural_note: Brits apologise constantly — and being late is one of the biggest sins. The unwritten rule: if you're more than 5 minutes late, you must text or call. Not warning someone is considered rude. When apologising, don't over-explain — a simple 'sorry, I'm running late' followed by how late you'll be is perfect. The response you'll almost always get: 'no worries!'

comprehension_questions.question: What will Marie do while she waits?

dialogue.line: Right, I'm starving. Lunch?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Definitely. What are you fancying?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Something quick. I've got thirty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Pret? It's just round the corner.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah, perfect. I'll get a sandwich.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Same. Maybe soup, actually. It's chilly.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Good shout. Want to split a brownie?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Always. Why do you even ask?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Just being polite.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Save your manners for work. Let's go.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You and a friend have a lunch break at work. Decide where to go, what to order, and whether to share something.

phrase_cle.example: — I'm starving! What shall we get? — Pret, obviously. It's right there.
phrase_cle.explanation: Гипербола. Буквально 'я голодаю', но в обычной речи просто означает 'я очень голоден'. Используется постоянно. Не путать с медицинским 'starvation' — в повседневном английском это просто сильное 'hungry'.

content.cultural_note: Pret A Manger ('Pret' for short) is the lunchtime king of London. Founded in 1986, there are now hundreds across the city — office workers live on their sandwiches, soups and coffee. 'Meal deal' lunches (sandwich + drink + snack for a set price) are a British institution. 'Good shout' is one of those phrases that instantly makes you sound like a local.

comprehension_questions.question: How much time does Sophie have?

comprehension_questions.hint: Pret — it's just round the corner.
comprehension_questions.question: Where are they going for lunch?

dialogue.line: Oh my God. Look outside.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It's absolutely chucking it down.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: And I forgot my brolly. Again.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You always forget your brolly.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know, I know. Maybe we stay here?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What, just stay in the café?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Until it stops. Another tea?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on then. I'm not getting soaked.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Look, it might clear up in twenty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Famous last words. I'll get the teas.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You and a friend are stuck somewhere because of rain. Complain about the weather, decide whether to wait it out, and make a plan.

phrase_cle.example: — Should we go out? — Have you looked outside? It's absolutely chucking it down.
phrase_cle.explanation: Очень разговорный способ сказать, что идёт сильный дождь. 'Chuck' = бросать, поэтому небо как будто 'швыряет' воду. 'Absolutely' добавляет интенсивности. Используется только в неформальной речи — с друзьями, в семье.

content.cultural_note: The British obsession with the weather is real — and rainy days are the national sport of complaining. London gets around 110 rainy days a year, so Brits have invented dozens of ways to describe rain: drizzle, spitting, pouring, chucking it down, bucketing, lashing. Always carry a 'brolly' — and expect to hear weather small talk at least three times a day.

comprehension_questions.hint: Stay in the café and wait it out.
comprehension_questions.question: What do they decide to do?

dialogue.line: Any plans for the weekend?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Not really. A lazy one, I hope.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same. But I might go to Borough Market on Saturday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Ooh, nice. Getting anything special?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Just a wander, really. Maybe some cheese.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Want company?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah, come along! Around eleven?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Let's say half eleven. I'm slow on weekends.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Half eleven it is. London Bridge station?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Perfect. Don't be late!
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: A friend asks about your weekend plans. Suggest meeting up for an activity, agree on a time and a place.

phrase_cle.example: — Any plans for the weekend? — Bit of a lazy one, hopefully. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный вопрос в пятницу — почти ритуал в британских офисах и между друзьями. В понедельник его зеркало: 'How was your weekend?'. Отвечать можно и подробно, и коротко ('not much, just chilling') — оба варианта норма.

content.cultural_note: Borough Market is London's most famous food market — near London Bridge, dating back to the 12th century. It's crowded, but for good reason: artisan cheese, fresh bread, street food from every corner of the world. Saturdays are busiest. 'Half eleven' confuses many learners — it's British for 11:30, not 10:30. In German it would mean half past ten, but in English 'half' comes after the hour.

key_vocabulary.note: гулять, заходить в магазины, не торопясь

comprehension_questions.question: Where is Sophie going on Saturday?

comprehension_questions.hint: Half eleven — 11:30 — at London Bridge station.
comprehension_questions.question: What time are they meeting?

dialogue.line: Oh no. No no no.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What? What happened?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: My brolly. Look.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh dear. That's completely broken.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You've got to be kidding me. I bought it yesterday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Where from?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That cheap shop near the station.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: There's your answer. Cheap brollies last one day.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Lesson learned. Got a spare?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Come under mine. Budge up.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Something small goes wrong while you're out with a friend (broken umbrella, spilled drink, lost glove). React, explain what happened, find a solution together.
prompt_phrases: You've got to be kidding me!

phrase_cle.phrase: You've got to be kidding me
phrase_cle.example: — The bus is cancelled. — You've got to be kidding me. It's freezing!
phrase_cle.explanation: Реакция на плохую/абсурдную новость. Выражает смесь удивления и раздражения. 'You've got to be' = 'you must be'. Интонация обычно падающая, с лёгким смехом. Используется повсеместно в разговоре.

content.cultural_note: Brits have a complicated relationship with umbrellas. Everyone needs one, no one can keep one. Cheap ones ('pound shop brollies') break within days. Windy London streets are especially brutal — you'll see dozens of abandoned broken umbrellas on any rainy day. 'Budge up' is a wonderfully British request to make room on a bench, sofa, or under an umbrella.

key_vocabulary.word: You've got to be kidding me

dialogue.line: I'm knackered. And hungry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Same. Fancy a cheeky Nando's?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh, absolutely. Shall we?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go on. There's one in Soho.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Perfect. Medium or hot?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I'm going mild today. I can't handle the spice.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Lightweight.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oi! It's been a long day.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Fair enough. Peri-peri chips, though?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Obviously. It's the law.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You're tired after work. Suggest going out for food with a friend. Pick a place, discuss what you'll order, tease each other a bit.

phrase_cle.example: — Coffee? — Shall we? I could do with a sit down.
phrase_cle.explanation: Короткое мягкое предложение. Заменяет полный вопрос вроде 'Shall we go?' или 'Shall we do it?'. Используется после того, как идея уже понятна из контекста. Звучит дружелюбно и необязывающе.

content.cultural_note: Nando's is a South African-Portuguese chicken chain — but in the UK, it's something else entirely: a cultural institution. 'A cheeky Nando's' became a viral phrase around 2014 and now means an unplanned, slightly indulgent visit. The chicken comes in spice levels: plain → mild → medium → hot → extra hot. Peri-peri chips are non-negotiable. Every young Brit has an opinion about Nando's.

comprehension_questions.question: How are Sophie and Marie feeling?

comprehension_questions.hint: Mild chicken — she can't handle the spice today.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie ordering?

dialogue.line: Where is this bus?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I've been here twenty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Typical TfL.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Let me check the app.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What does it say?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Due in... nine minutes. Again.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh, come on. It said nine minutes ten minutes ago.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Classic. Shall we just walk?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How far is it?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Twenty minutes on foot. Worth it for the sanity.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You and a friend are waiting for a bus that isn't coming. Check the app, express frustration, and decide together what to do — keep waiting, walk, or take a different route.

phrase_cle.example: — The bus hasn't come for twenty minutes. — Typical TfL. Nothing new.
phrase_cle.explanation: TfL = Transport for London, компания, управляющая автобусами и метро. 'Typical TfL' говорят с лёгким раздражением, когда что-то идёт не так: задержки, отмены, забастовки. Лондонцы обожают жаловаться на транспорт — это часть городской идентичности.
phrase_cle.translation: Как всегда в транспорте / в TfL

content.cultural_note: Complaining about public transport is a core London pastime. TfL runs everything: the Tube, buses, trains, even bikes ('Santander Cycles'). The 'TfL Go' app shows live bus arrivals — but everyone knows the promised time can keep rolling back. 'Worth it for the sanity' is a phrase Londoners use to justify walking instead of waiting — long walks through central London are part of the lifestyle.

comprehension_questions.question: How long has Marie been waiting?

comprehension_questions.hint: The bus is due in nine minutes — but it's been saying that for a while.
comprehension_questions.question: What does the app say?
What do they decide to do?

dialogue.line: Fancy a Sunday roast tomorrow?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, yes please. I haven''t had one in ages.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The Crown does a really good one.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Is that the pub on your road?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah, that one. How does one o''clock sound?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Perfect. Beef or chicken?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Beef, obviously.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Obviously. And Yorkshires.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Goes without saying.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I''m excited already. See you tomorrow!
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Invite a friend for a meal at a specific place. Agree on time, food choices, and details. Use ''How does that sound?'' to check they''re happy with your plan.
prompt_phrases: The [place] does a really good one.

phrase_cle.example: — The pub at one, roast, pints? How does that sound? — Sounds perfect.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный способ предложить план и узнать мнение собеседника. Буквально — 'как это звучит?'. Используется, когда ты уже сформулировал предложение и хочешь услышать одобрение или возражение. Работает и в неформальных, и в полуформальных ситуациях.

content.cultural_note: The Sunday roast is sacred British tradition: roast meat (usually beef), roast potatoes, Yorkshire puddings, gravy, vegetables. Served in pubs between 12 and 4pm on Sundays. Pub roasts are a national obsession — people queue, book in advance, even argue over which pub does the best one. Yorkshire puddings (hollow, crispy savoury pastries) are non-negotiable. ''The Crown'' is one of the most common pub names in England.

key_vocabulary.note: согласие, что спорить не о чем

comprehension_questions.hint: A Sunday roast — a traditional British meal.
comprehension_questions.question: What are Sophie and Marie planning?

comprehension_questions.hint: The Crown pub, on Sophie''s road.
comprehension_questions.question: Where are they going?
What does Sophie say about Yorkshires?

dialogue.line: So, Emma''s birthday is next Friday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, I forgot! What are we doing?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I was thinking dinner somewhere nice.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good idea. Italian? She loves pasta.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Exactly what I thought. That place in Covent Garden?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Perfect. Should we book?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah, Friday''s busy. I''ll call them.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What about a card?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I''ll pick one up. Want to split it?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Always. You''re a star for organising.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You''re planning a friend''s birthday with another friend. Suggest a place, agree on details, split the responsibilities (booking, card, gift).

phrase_cle.example: — I was thinking, maybe we could do a picnic? — Oh, that''s a nice idea!
phrase_cle.explanation: Мягкий способ ввести предложение или идею. Используется, когда не хочешь давить — даёшь собеседнику понять, что идея предварительная и обсуждаемая. Супер-полезная конструкция в любом разговоре, особенно когда что-то планируешь вместе.

content.cultural_note: Covent Garden is one of London''s most charming areas — old market buildings, street performers, restaurants everywhere. Italian food is massively popular in the UK. When planning a birthday, Brits often split costs (card, gift, dinner bill) between friends — this is called ''going halves'' or ''splitting it''. It''s polite to offer, weird to insist on paying entirely.

key_vocabulary.translation: Именно об этом я и думала

comprehension_questions.hint: An Italian restaurant in Covent Garden.
comprehension_questions.question: Where will they go?

dialogue.line: Sophie, I need to tell you something.

dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She''ll understand. Have a lovely weekend!
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: You need to cancel plans with a friend. Tell them honestly, give a reason, apologise briefly, and offer a way to make up for it (card, message, another date).
prompt_phrases: I need to tell you something.

phrase_cle.example: — Coming to the party? — Sorry, I can''t make it. Got work stuff.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартная вежливая формулировка отмены. ''Make it'' здесь — идиома ''добраться, успеть''. Звучит менее резко, чем ''I can''t come''. Хорошо работает в любой ситуации — дружеской, рабочей, формальной.

content.cultural_note: Brits cancel plans with layers of apology — it''s almost an art form. The magic phrase ''I can''t make it'' softens the blow. Family obligations are the most accepted reason to bail: ''family comes first'' is almost sacred. In return, a cancelled friend is expected to respond graciously with ''don''t worry'' or ''no problem''. Holding a grudge over a cancellation is considered childish — as long as you don''t do it too often.

comprehension_questions.hint: She can''t come to Emma''s birthday dinner.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie telling Sophie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Her cousin is visiting — only this weekend.
comprehension_questions.question: Why can''t she come?

dialogue.line: Marie, I owe you an apology.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What for?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I forgot about our coffee yesterday.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh! I was there for twenty minutes.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I''m so sorry. My head is a mess.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What happened? Are you okay?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Just work stuff. No excuse, though.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Don''t worry. Let me know next time.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Can I buy you lunch to make up for it?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Well, I won''t say no.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You forgot a plan with a friend. Apologise properly: acknowledge what you did, take responsibility, offer to make up for it.
prompt_phrases: Can I [something nice] to make up for it?

phrase_cle.example: — I forgot your birthday. — What?! — I''m so sorry, honestly.
phrase_cle.explanation: Усиленное ''sorry''. ''So'' делает извинение искренним, а не формальным. Используется после ошибки, проступка, забытого обещания. Если британец говорит ''I''m so sorry'' — это не пустая вежливость, а реальное сожаление.

content.cultural_note: Brits take apologies seriously — but also don''t like it when they drag on. The ritual: acknowledge, don''t over-explain, offer to make up for it. ''I''m so sorry'' + brief reason + ''let me buy you a coffee/lunch'' is the classic formula. Over-apologising is almost worse than under-apologising — it becomes performative. The response ''don''t worry'' is the social signal that the issue is closed — dwell on it longer and you''ll make things awkward.

comprehension_questions.question: How does Sophie offer to make up for it?

dialogue.line: Hey Marie! How''s things?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, hi! Good, yeah. Bit tired.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Are you eating properly?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Not really. I keep ordering in.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Marie! Come on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I know, I know. Work''s mad.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Want me to come over Saturday? I''ll cook.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Would you? That would be lovely.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Of course. I''ll bring wine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You''re the best. Love you.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: Call a friend you haven''t spoken to in a while. Ask how they''re doing, notice if they''re stressed, offer to help in a caring way.

phrase_cle.example: — Hey, how''s things? — Not bad, not bad. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Более разговорный, тёплый вариант ''How are you?''. Буквально — ''как все вещи?''. Используется между близкими друзьями или семьёй. ''How''s things'' звучит естественно и уютно — как будто заходишь в гости.

content.cultural_note: Phone calls between close friends in the UK are becoming rarer (most young Brits prefer texting or voice notes) — but when they happen, they''re often warm and caring. ''Are you eating properly?'' is the classic friend/mum question. Offering to come over and cook for a stressed friend is a major gesture of care. Wine is the traditional contribution. ''Lovely'' is one of the most-used British adjectives — soft, warm, genuinely positive.

comprehension_questions.hint: A bit tired — and not eating properly.
comprehension_questions.question: How is Marie feeling?

comprehension_questions.hint: To come over on Saturday and cook.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Sophie offering?

dialogue.line: Ooh, look at those ducks!
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: So cute. Right, I''ve got news.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Go on.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: You''ll never guess what Emma told me.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What?!
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: She''s seeing someone new. From her gym.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: No way! Tell me everything.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: His name''s Tom, he''s a teacher.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh, she deserves this. Good for her.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right? I''m so happy for her.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You''re in the park with a friend and you''ve got good gossip about a mutual friend. Build up the story with intrigue, share the news, react together positively.

phrase_cle.example: — You''ll never guess what Emma told me. — Oh, go on.
phrase_cle.explanation: Классическое британское начало любой сплетни или интересной истории. Создаёт интригу. Собеседник обычно отвечает ''What?'' или ''Go on!'' — и ты рассказываешь. Без этой фразы ни одна уважающая себя британская сплетня не начинается.
phrase_cle.translation: Ни за что не угадаешь что

content.cultural_note: London''s parks are social spaces — people actually meet there to chat, not just exercise. Hyde Park, Regent''s Park, Clapham Common — all crowded on sunny weekends with groups of friends sitting on the grass. Gossip (when it''s kind, not cruel) is a bonding ritual in British friendship. ''Deserves this'' is an important concept: friends genuinely want to see each other happy, and news about love, jobs, or successes is celebrated as if it''s their own.

comprehension_questions.hint: In a park — Marie mentions ducks.
comprehension_questions.question: Where are Sophie and Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Emma is seeing someone new — Tom, from her gym.
comprehension_questions.question: What''s the gossip?
How do they feel about it?

dialogue.line: Marie, can I ask you a favour?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Of course. What''s up?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I''m away next week. Could you feed my cat?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, easy. Where are you going?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Bristol. Work thing.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Nice. How long will you be gone?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Five days. I''ll leave food and a key.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: No problem. Does Luna like strangers?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She loves you, don''t be silly.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Awww. Leave me your number just in case.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You''re going away for a few days. Ask a friend for a small favour (feed a pet, water plants, accept a package). Explain details clearly and thank them.
prompt_phrases: Can I ask you a favour?

phrase_cle.phrase: Can I ask you a favour?
phrase_cle.example: — Can I ask you a favour? — Of course, go on. — Could you water my plants next week?
phrase_cle.explanation: Вежливая прелюдия перед просьбой. Даёт собеседнику понять, что дальше будет просьба, и даёт ему пространство сразу сказать да или нет. Почти всегда после этого следует ''of course'' или ''sure'' — но привычка такая, британская.

content.cultural_note: Bristol is a lively city in South West England — often topping lists of ''best places to live''. Asking friends to pet-sit, water plants, or accept deliveries is a normal part of friendship in the UK. Giving a spare key is a sign of trust, and Brits take it seriously — if someone gives you their key, you''re officially inner-circle. When doing a favour, the correct response is always ''no problem'' or ''easy'' — never ''you owe me''.

key_vocabulary.word: Can I ask you a favour?

comprehension_questions.hint: Someone to feed her cat while she''s away.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie need?

dialogue.line: How did it go?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Fine. She was really nice.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: What did she say?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: It''s just a bad cold. Nothing serious.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, thank God. You had me worried.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: She said rest and fluids.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Classic. Did she give you anything?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Just paracetamol. Nothing strong.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. Let''s get you home.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Honestly, you''re a lifesaver.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: A friend meets you after a doctor''s appointment. Tell them how it went, what the doctor said, and what treatment you got. Reassure them it''s not serious.

phrase_cle.example: — How are you today? — Not feeling great, to be honest.
phrase_cle.explanation: Мягкий, нормальный способ сказать, что плохо себя чувствуешь. Не драматично (не ''I''m dying''), но и не преуменьшает (не ''I''m fine''). Идеально для разговоров с врачом, с коллегами, с друзьями. Можно добавить детали: ''a bit tired'', ''dizzy'', ''queasy''.

content.cultural_note: GP stands for ''General Practitioner'' — the first doctor you see in the UK''s National Health Service (NHS). Healthcare is free, but appointments can be hard to get. The running joke: whatever you have, the GP will tell you ''rest and fluids'' and send you home with paracetamol. It''s mostly true — British medicine tends to be cautious about stronger drugs. A friend meeting you after an appointment is a common kindness, especially if you''ve been worried.

comprehension_questions.question: What medicine did the doctor prescribe?

dialogue.line: Platform 14. We''ve got ages.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Good. I hate rushing for trains.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Me too. Better safe than sorry.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Shall we grab a coffee?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yes. And maybe a croissant?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Obviously. How long is the journey?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: About an hour. Not bad.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Perfect. I love Brighton.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: The sea, the pier, fish and chips...
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Stop, you''re making me hungry!
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You''re at a train station with a friend, about to travel. Check the platform and time, decide to grab something to eat, talk about where you''re going.

phrase_cle.example: — You''re two hours early! — Better safe than sorry.
phrase_cle.explanation: Британская мудрость по жизни. Используется когда делаешь что-то осторожное, хотя может и не обязательно. Например, берёшь зонт в ясный день, приходишь раньше на поезд, покупаешь билет заранее. Обычная фраза, звучит по-взрослому и рассудительно.

content.cultural_note: Brighton is the most famous seaside town in England — an hour south of London by train. Pebble beach (not sandy, Brits love complaining about this), the iconic Brighton Pier with old-school amusements, fish and chips, bohemian atmosphere. It''s called ''London-by-the-Sea''. Victoria Station is the main departure point from London. ''Getting the train'' for a day trip is a British institution — especially in summer.

key_vocabulary.note: любая поездка из А в Б

comprehension_questions.question: Which platform is the train from?
How long is the journey to Brighton?

comprehension_questions.hint: The sea, the pier, and fish and chips.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie like about Brighton?

dialogue.line: Hi, have you got anything for a sore throat?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: How bad is it?
dialogue.speaker: Pharmacist

dialogue.line: Not too bad. Just scratchy.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: These lozenges are very good.
dialogue.speaker: Pharmacist

dialogue.line: How often should I take them?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Every three hours, with food.
dialogue.speaker: Pharmacist

dialogue.line: Perfect. And a bottle of water, please.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That''s six pounds forty altogether.
dialogue.speaker: Pharmacist

dialogue.line: Can I pay by card?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Of course. Hope you feel better soon!
dialogue.speaker: Pharmacist

pratique.scenario: You have a minor health problem (sore throat, cold, headache). Go to a pharmacy and ask for help. Describe your symptom, ask how to take the medicine, pay.
prompt_phrases: How often should I take them?

phrase_cle.example: — Have you got anything for a sore throat? — Yes, these lozenges work well.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартная фраза в аптеке. ''Have you got'' — британский вариант ''do you have''. После этого называешь симптом или проблему. Фармацевт порекомендует лекарство. Очень частотная конструкция в бытовом английском.

content.cultural_note: Boots is the UK''s biggest pharmacy chain — you''ll find one on almost every high street. British pharmacists can advise on minor illnesses without a doctor''s note, and often recommend paracetamol, ibuprofen, or throat lozenges for common problems. Paying by card is standard everywhere — even for small amounts. The pharmacist''s parting line ''hope you feel better soon'' is a warm, customary goodbye in medical settings.

comprehension_questions.hint: She has a sore throat — a bit scratchy.
comprehension_questions.question: What''s wrong with Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Lozenges — every three hours, with food.
comprehension_questions.question: What does the pharmacist recommend?

dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at this cheese stall.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I know. The smell is incredible.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Can I try a bit of the cheddar?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Of course, love. This one is three years aged.
dialogue.speaker: Stallholder

dialogue.line: Mmm. That is good.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: We''ll take two hundred grams, please.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Lovely. Anything else?
dialogue.speaker: Stallholder

dialogue.line: Do you have any fresh bread?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Next stall along, the sourdough is brilliant.
dialogue.speaker: Stallholder

dialogue.line: Perfect. This is my happy place.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You''re at a food market. Approach a stall, ask to try something, compliment it, ask about related products at other stalls.

phrase_cle.example: — That cheese looks amazing. — Can I try a bit? — Of course, here you go.
phrase_cle.explanation: Волшебная фраза на любом рынке с едой. На британских рынках продавцы обычно с радостью дают попробовать — это часть культуры. ''A bit'' звучит скромно, не жадно. Используется для сыра, оливок, чатни, мёда, колбас — всего что можно попробовать.

content.cultural_note: Borough Market, near London Bridge, is England''s most famous food market — it''s been there in some form since the 1000s. Artisan cheese, sourdough bread, Spanish charcuterie, street food from everywhere. Stallholders calling you ''love'' or ''darling'' is friendly British retail culture, not flirting — totally normal. Trying before you buy is standard. Saturdays are crowded but magical. Budget extra time: you''ll eat your way through the market.

comprehension_questions.question: What are Sophie and Marie buying?
How much cheese do they buy?

dialogue.line: Hi, there''s a problem with my card.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Okay, what happened?
dialogue.speaker: Bank clerk

dialogue.line: It was declined this morning. Twice.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Let me have a look. Your name?
dialogue.speaker: Bank clerk

dialogue.line: Sophie Clarke.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Right... ah, the card is blocked.
dialogue.speaker: Bank clerk

dialogue.line: Blocked? Why?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Suspicious activity. Someone tried to use it in Madrid.
dialogue.speaker: Bank clerk

dialogue.line: I''ve never been to Madrid!
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Exactly. Good thing we caught it. I''ll issue a new card.
dialogue.speaker: Bank clerk

pratique.scenario: Your bank card has a problem. Go to the bank (or call), explain the issue, give your details, find out what the bank will do next.
prompt_phrases: There''s a problem with my card.

phrase_cle.phrase: There''s a problem with my card
phrase_cle.example: — How can I help? — There''s a problem with my card. It was declined.
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартная фраза при любой проблеме с банковской картой — не работает, заблокирована, потеряна. Нейтральная, не драматичная. Идёт с сотрудниками банка, call-центром, кассирами. Простая конструкция ''there''s a problem with [X]'' работает для любой проблемы.

content.cultural_note: British banks are generally good at fraud detection and block cards quickly when suspicious activity appears — abroad purchases especially. If you''re travelling, it''s smart to tell your bank beforehand (''travel notification'') or your card may be declined even for legitimate spending. When a card is blocked, getting a replacement usually takes 3-5 working days. Major banks: HSBC, Barclays, Lloyds, NatWest, Santander — they''re all on every high street.

comprehension_questions.hint: Suspicious activity — someone tried to use it in Madrid.
comprehension_questions.question: Why was the card blocked?

dialogue.line: Hi, I need to send this to France.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What''s inside?
dialogue.speaker: Clerk

dialogue.line: A book and a small gift.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Worth more than fifty pounds?
dialogue.speaker: Clerk

dialogue.line: No, about twenty.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Right. Standard or tracked?
dialogue.speaker: Clerk

dialogue.line: How long will it take?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Standard — five to seven days. Tracked — three.
dialogue.speaker: Clerk

dialogue.line: Tracked, please. Can I pay by card?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Of course. That''ll be twelve pounds.
dialogue.speaker: Clerk

pratique.scenario: You need to send a parcel abroad. Explain what''s inside, answer the value question, choose between standard and tracked, pay.
prompt_phrases: I need to send this to [country].

phrase_cle.example: — How long will it take? — Three to five working days.
phrase_cle.explanation: Базовый вопрос про сроки доставки, ожидания в очереди, выполнения заказа. Универсальная фраза на почте, в ресторане, в мастерской. ''It'' относится к процессу, о котором говорите. Краткое, вежливое, практичное — и слышишь каждый день.

content.cultural_note: Royal Mail is the UK''s main postal service — though private couriers like DPD and Evri (formerly Hermes) are also popular. Post offices now sit inside newsagents and small shops — the big standalone branches are disappearing. Sending abroad from the UK isn''t cheap: a small parcel to Europe starts around £10-12. Tracked services cost more but give you peace of mind — a very British concept. Christmas time is chaos; post early if you want it to arrive on time.

comprehension_questions.hint: A book and a small gift.
comprehension_questions.question: What is Marie sending?

dialogue.line: Can I ask you something a bit personal?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Go for it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Do you actually like your job?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Honestly? Not really.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Same here. It''s okay, but...
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: But what?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I don''t know. I want to do something more creative.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Oh, what would you do?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Maybe design. Or writing. One day.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: One day soon, I hope.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You''re having an honest conversation with a close friend about work. Ask if they''re happy, share your own feelings, talk about dreams or changes you''d like to make.
prompt_phrases: Can I ask you something personal?
Do you actually like your job?

phrase_cle.example: — So, what do you do? — I''m a teacher. You?
phrase_cle.explanation: Стандартный британский способ спросить о профессии. ''Do'' здесь означает ''делать для работы''. Простой, универсальный — звучит на вечеринках, знакомствах, собеседованиях. В ответ не нужна длинная история — короткая формулировка работает лучше всего.

content.cultural_note: ''What do you do?'' is one of the first questions Brits ask when meeting — even more than ''where are you from?''. But it''s not judgmental: people talk openly about job dissatisfaction. ''I''m between jobs'' or ''I''m doing X while I figure things out'' are completely acceptable answers. Making a career change is common and respected — the idea that you should do one thing forever is outdated. Dreaming aloud about creative work is normal friendship conversation, not showing off.

comprehension_questions.hint: Honestly, not really — she doesn''t love it.
comprehension_questions.question: How does Marie feel about her job?

comprehension_questions.hint: Something more creative — maybe design or writing.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Sophie want to do instead?
What does Marie say at the end?

dialogue.line: So? What did you think?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Honestly? A bit long.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh really? I loved it.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The ending was beautiful, I''ll give you that.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I cried three times.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Three?! Sophie, it was fine.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: You have no heart.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I have taste. Come on, chips?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Obviously. But we''re debating this further.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I was worried you''d say that.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You just saw a film or went to a concert with a friend. Share opinions, disagree playfully, tease each other about your different tastes.

phrase_cle.example: — Well? What did you think? — Honestly? A bit long.
phrase_cle.explanation: Универсальный вопрос после совместного опыта — кино, ресторан, концерт, встреча. Приглашение поделиться мнением. Краткий, вежливый, предполагает честный ответ. Ответы могут быть очень разные: ''loved it'', ''not for me'', ''bit slow'', ''absolute rubbish''.

content.cultural_note: Odeon and Vue are the UK''s biggest cinema chains — you''ll find them in almost every city. Cinema culture is alive in Britain: premiere nights, arthouse indies, classic re-releases. Brits love debating films afterwards — in pubs, over chips, walking home. Disagreement is part of the fun: a friend who calls you ''no heart'' is joking, not fighting. And of course — chips after the cinema is tradition. Fish and chips if you''re near the sea, just chips otherwise.

comprehension_questions.hint: No — Sophie loved it, Marie thought it was long.
comprehension_questions.question: Did Sophie and Marie agree about the film?
How many times did Sophie cry?

comprehension_questions.hint: Getting chips and continuing the debate.
comprehension_questions.question: What are they doing after the film?

dialogue.line: You look awful. What happened?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I''ve had a rubbish day.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Oh no. Tea?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Please.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Tell me everything.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: My boss. He was awful today.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What did he do?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Shouted at me in front of everyone.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: That''s not on, Marie.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: I just want to forget about it.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: A friend has had a terrible day. Notice they look upset, offer comfort (tea!), listen to their story, and show you''re on their side.

phrase_cle.example: — How was your day? — Ugh. Rubbish, honestly.
phrase_cle.explanation: ''Rubbish'' — очень британский способ сказать ''плохо'', ''дрянь''. Звучит честно, эмоционально, но не слишком драматично. Более мягкий вариант — ''a rough day''. Используется в тёплых, дружеских разговорах — когда хочется выговориться.

content.cultural_note: When a British friend is having a bad day, the ritual is: tea first, then listening. Tea isn''t really about thirst — it''s a ceremony of care. ''That''s not on'' is a brilliantly British expression of moral disapproval — softer than ''that''s outrageous'' but stronger than ''that''s unfair''. It draws a clear line: this behaviour is not acceptable. Good friends respond with outrage on your behalf, not with ''well, maybe you did something wrong...''. Loyalty first, analysis later.

comprehension_questions.hint: Her boss shouted at her in front of everyone at work.
comprehension_questions.question: Why is Marie upset?
What does Marie want to do?

dialogue.line: Sophie! I can''t find my phone.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: What? Where did you have it last?
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: The taxi. Oh God, the taxi.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Okay, don''t panic. Call it from mine.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: It''s ringing! Someone''s picking up!
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Hello?
dialogue.speaker: Taxi driver

dialogue.line: Hi! I think I left my phone in your cab.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Yeah, it''s here on the back seat. Shall I drop it back?
dialogue.speaker: Taxi driver

dialogue.line: That would be amazing. Thank you so much.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: See? Always some good people around.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: You''ve lost your phone. Call a friend in a panic, retrace your steps, work out where it is, and contact whoever has it.
prompt_phrases: Where did you have it last?
That would be amazing. Thank you so much.

phrase_cle.example: — Sophie! I can''t find my phone. — Okay, calm down. Where did you last see it?
phrase_cle.explanation: Простая конструкция для любой потерянной вещи: ''I can''t find my [X]''. Работает с ключами, очками, кошельком. В британской культуре паника вокруг потерянного телефона — социально приемлема, даже ожидаема. Друзья обычно сразу включаются в поиск.

content.cultural_note: London black cabs (taxis) have a famous reputation for honesty — drivers often return lost property. Uber drivers too, though you''ll need to contact them through the app. The Find My iPhone / Find My Device apps can pinpoint lost phones — but a classic call-and-listen often works. When a stranger does you a kindness, the British response is overflow with thanks: ''amazing'', ''legend'', ''you''ve saved my life''. Never just ''thanks''. Offering to pay a little extra (like a tip) is appropriate and polite.

key_vocabulary.note: основной совет Marie в любой ситуации

dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: I''m terrified, honestly.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Terrified is normal. Let''s go celebrate!
dialogue.speaker: Marie

pratique.scenario: You''ve got good news to share with a close friend. Build up the moment, share the news, share your real feelings (nervous, scared, excited), let them celebrate you.
prompt_phrases: I''ve got something to tell you.

phrase_cle.phrase: I''ve got something to tell you
phrase_cle.example: — Listen, I''ve got something to tell you. — Oh God, is it bad?
phrase_cle.explanation: Серьёзная прелюдия перед важной новостью — хорошей или плохой. Заставляет собеседника сесть и слушать. Интонация решает всё: радостная — хорошая новость, серьёзная — плохая. В любом случае после этой фразы в воздухе появляется напряжение.

content.cultural_note: Brits celebrate career milestones seriously — but also with a self-deprecating edge. ''I''m terrified'' is the honest, humble reaction to good news; saying ''I deserve this, I''m amazing'' would be seen as arrogant. A friend''s response: genuine pride (''proud of you'') + practical comfort (''terrified is normal'') + immediate celebration (pub, dinner, champagne). The British celebration of choice: a pub. Or a fancier place if the news is really big. ''Let''s go celebrate'' is a standard, warm, non-negotiable response.

key_vocabulary.word: I''ve got something to tell you

comprehension_questions.hint: She got the design job she wanted.
comprehension_questions.question: What''s Sophie''s news?

comprehension_questions.hint: Terrified, honestly — but it''s good news.
comprehension_questions.question: How is she feeling?

dialogue.line: Edinburgh was a brilliant idea.

dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Do you remember our first coffee in that queue?
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Feels like ages ago.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

dialogue.line: Here''s to all our coffees. And all the ones to come.
dialogue.speaker: Marie

dialogue.line: Cheers, Marie. To us.
dialogue.speaker: Sophie

pratique.scenario: You''re at a beautiful place with a close friend. Take in the view, remember your shared history, raise a toast to your friendship.

phrase_cle.example: — Look at that view, Marie. — I know. It''s ridiculous.
phrase_cle.explanation: Простая, от сердца. Используется в моменты, когда пейзаж или зрелище действительно захватывает. ''That'' подчёркивает эмоциональную силу — не просто ''this view'', а ''что за вид''. Звучит на вершинах гор, на пляжах, в смотровых площадках. Финальная фраза A1 курса — потому что когда ты можешь просто выдохнуть и сказать это, ты уже говоришь по-английски.

content.cultural_note: Arthur''s Seat is an extinct volcano in the middle of Edinburgh — you can hike to the top in about 45 minutes and see all of the city, the sea, and the hills beyond. It''s a tradition for friends visiting Edinburgh together. ''Here''s to [something]'' is the classic British toast — raising a glass (or a takeaway coffee, or just your hand) to a person, an occasion, or a memory. Ending the A1 journey here is intentional: you''ve moved from ordering coffee in a London queue to toasting your friendship on a Scottish hilltop. That''s fluency beginning.

comprehension_questions.hint: Edinburgh — at the top of Arthur''s Seat.
comprehension_questions.question: Where are Sophie and Marie?

comprehension_questions.hint: Their first coffee in the queue — their very first conversation.
comprehension_questions.question: What does Marie remember?
